explicit_text: I was born in Port Angeles, Washington, which used to be a milltown. Growing up there shaped much of my early life, especially since my father was always working on projects and extra jobs around our home. It was a place where I started working young, selling newspapers and helping out with various tasks. Although it's no longer a milltown, my roots there remain important to me.

Although the town has changed and is no longer centered around mills, my roots there remain important to me.

explicit_text: When I was eleven, I lived away from home for the summer and worked on a charter fishing boat at La Push, Washington. That summer was probably the best of my life, giving me a taste of independence and hard work at a young age. It was an early job that helped form my character and work ethic.

explicit_text: I started reading philosophy when I was in grade school, and Plato was a big influence on me. Through his works, I learned about the Socratic method, which shaped my thinking and approach to debate and ideas. His philosophy stayed with me as I grew older and engaged in political discussions.

explicit_text: During junior high in the early 1960s, the Vietnam War was heating up and it sparked debates at my school about capitalism versus communism. I volunteered to debate for the merits of communism and even won, which led to me being sent to the school psychiatrist. Later, I became deeply involved in antiwar organizing, including protests and distributing literature, especially around 1966 and the early 1970s.

entity: West Coast Pulp and Paper Workers Union
explicit_text: My girlfriend's mother worked for the West Coast Pulp and Paper Workers Union, which was breaking away from their international union at the time. They had an old mimeograph machine that I used to print an antiwar newsletter. This connection helped me gain experience in radical union work and political activism during my youth.

explicit_text: One year after the invasion of Cambodia in 1970, my friend and I traveled to Washington, D.C. to participate in antiwar protests. We spent a month there collecting petitions for the People's Peace Treaty and supporting demonstrators. The Cambodian Campaign was a pivotal event that intensified our activism and led to our arrest during mass protests.

explicit_text: In 1971, I spent a month in Washington, D.C. during a large wave of antiwar protests. I worked in an office that provided housing for demonstrators, helping to accommodate about a million people. During this time, I was arrested in Mount Vernon Square along with thousands of others, and experienced firsthand the tense confrontations with the National Guard and police.

explicit_text: I was arrested in Mount Vernon Square during the 1971 antiwar protests in Washington, D.C. We were held in a small fenced park, where I remember an elderly black man joining us in solidarity and getting arrested as well. It was a powerful moment of community and resistance during a very turbulent time.

explicit_text: During the 1971 protests in Washington, D.C., the National Guard played a significant role in containing demonstrators. We were held in a football field surrounded by barbed wire and National Guard troops, and they even used teargas on us. It was a tense and intimidating experience, but it also showed the lengths authorities would go to suppress the antiwar movement.

explicit_text: After a severe injury at a lumber mill, I decided not to return to work and instead joined a pacifist community in Philadelphia. I was inspired by a book about a Quaker peace community there, but my experience was disappointing because the community's radical lifestyles didn't match my blue-collar background. Eventually, I left Philadelphia to return to school and pursue chemistry.

explicit_text: I was awarded a scholarship in chemical engineering to the University of Washington, but unfortunately, the scholarship was given away by the time I arrived. Later in life, after leaving Boeing, I volunteered on a project there studying diatoms in Puget Sound to better understand the local ecosystem. The University played a role both in my early academic ambitions and later environmental interests.

explicit_text: I worked at Boeing for 25 years, starting without a degree but with a strong recommendation from a Ph.D. colleague. It was an old-time American company that valued skill and initiative, allowing me to work on projects like the Hubble Space Telescope and environmental monitoring. Later, I transitioned to forensic science work while still at Boeing, which was very satisfying and took me around the world.

explicit_text: One of the projects I was proud to work on at Boeing was the Hubble Space Telescope. I was the first employee in their environmental laboratory, monitoring effluents from their sites, and contributed to materials and manufacturing processes related to the telescope. It was a highlight of my career and a source of great satisfaction.

explicit_text: In my forensic science work, I handled a case involving a company executive who was thought to have been poisoned in a hotel room in Mongolia. By analyzing a pinch of dust, I discovered that insecticide sprayed by hotel staff was the cause of the illness. This case was one of many that took me around the world and showcased the unique nature of my work.

explicit_text: After leaving Boeing, I volunteered on a project at the University of Washington studying diatoms in Puget Sound. My goal was to identify all the species living in this inland waterway, which had not been systematically studied before. This work is important because plankton like diatoms produce a significant portion of the world's oxygen, and their decline is a serious environmental concern.

explicit_text: I believe the decline in plankton populations, such as diatoms, is a much bigger problem than the loss of the Amazon rainforest. While the rainforest gets a lot of attention, the microscopic life in the oceans and inland waters produces the majority of the oxygen we breathe. Unfortunately, there is very little funding or awareness for this critical issue.

explicit_text: My father was from New Orleans and left when he was 18 because he hated racism. He had worked for a jeweler on Canal Street and lived a relatively fancy lifestyle with tuxedos and big band music. His stories about New Orleans and the people he worked with influenced my early life and understanding of racial and social issues.
